input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin under study is a laboratory‑scale gantry crane that incorporates a mechanical frame with linear rails, a hoist, a J‑aw, and an electronic motion controller. The crane operates in a controlled laboratory environment and is specifically chosen for its representative industrial‑grade kinematic topology, making it an ideal candidate for a detailed digital twin that requires precise CAD modeling, kinematic simulation, and real‑time sensor acquisition.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides a range of services including: • Continuous monitoring of the physical crane via real‑time sensor streams from the controller; • Optimal control through model‑based trajectory optimisation that reduces energy consumption and wear; • Historical data logging where all sensor outputs are timestamped and stored for later analysis; • Interactive data visualization via a web interface that renders 3‑D CAD models and displays time‑series plots; • Continuous validation by updating the kinematic model against live measurements to detect drift or faults.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The exemplar is built exclusively with freely available, commonly used software. CAD models are represented in standard formats (e.g., STEP, STL) and imported into an open‑source kinematic modelling framework for simulation. Data ingestion, storage, and logging rely on standard databases (e.g., SQLite or PostgreSQL), while visualisation and interaction are handled by open‑source libraries such as Plotly or Matplotlib, all coordinated through a Python‑based workflow. Web‑based interfaces and APIs expose the twin services, ensuring reproducibility and ease of extension for future research.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Each exemplar implements a one‑to‑one mapping between a single physical system and a single Digital Twin instance. The architecture is decentralized per system: no coordination or aggregation layer between Digital Twins is described in the documents. The responsibility scope of each instance is limited to monitoring, adapting, and visualising its corresponding physical counterpart, with no shared global state or inter‑Twin synchronization mechanisms mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The overall orchestration is built around containerization or virtualization for easy deployment. In GreenhouseDT, the stack consists of a virtual‑machine image that bundles an InfluxDB time‑series database for sensor data and an ActiveMQ message broker for real‑time data flow. Air Quality Management leverages Microsoft Azure services (IoT Hub, Azure Functions, and Azure Stream Analytics) to ingest sensor streams from a Raspberry‑Pi and drive an AutomationML‐based Digital Twin model; the same pattern uses a cloud‑hosted message bus (Service Bus) for cross‑service communication. The gantry crane example references containerized deployment but does not specify a concrete orchestration platform. Overall, the system employs a central message bus (ActiveMQ or Azure Service Bus), persistent data stores (InfluxDB or Azure Table/Blob), and containerized application components running on virtual machines or cloud instances.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is performed primarily via message passing protocols: ActiveMQ for on‑premises instrumentation in GreenhouseDT, Azure IoT Hub/Service Bus for Azure‑based ingestion in Air Quality Management, and AutomationML as an interoperability standard for semantic modeling. Data is exchanged in JSON or binary formats over MQTT/AMQP, and the Digital Twins expose REST/HTTPS APIs for platform integration. The dashboards and simulation engines consume the published data streams; no direct integration with third‑party enterprise systems is described beyond the cloud services and message brokers cited.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Digital Twin collects real‑time crane state data from the Physical Crane Interface, a Python program that connects directly to the crane controllers. The Logging Application continuously streams sensor measurements – including position, velocity, and actuator status – into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. Data are transmitted over a local network using the native PostgreSQL wire protocol, with each record written as a binary tuple. The ingestion is continuous, ensuring that the Simulation Service and Continuous Validation Service receive up-to-date operational data for trajectory comparison and monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate in the Control Application, which takes operator orders from the Human Machine Interface. Commands are encoded as high‑level movement trajectories and passed through the Physical Crane Interface to the physical crane controllers via a Python API over TCP. The Digital Twin issues these commands without explicit authentication or verification steps, and the Physical Crane Interface forwards them to the hardware where they are executed. No explicit feedback loop or command‑execution confirmation mechanism is described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication within the Digital Twin is orchestrated by an MQTT broker, which publishes and subscribes to topics for coordination between the Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, and Dashboard services. The Physical Crane Interface and Logging Application communicate over the local network using TCP, with data persisted through PostgreSQL’s wire protocol to TimescaleDB. The documents do not specify bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security protocols beyond the use of MQTT and TCP.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All Digital Twin containers (Python services, TimescaleDB, Grafana dashboard, MQTT broker) are deployed on the same on‑premises host that houses the physical crane, as indicated by the direct use of local network connections and a single TimescaleDB instance. No explicit cloud or edge deployment, scalability strategy, or availability mechanisms are mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Air Quality Management exemplar follows a model‑driven engineering approach. The digital twin is defined using AutomationML, a standard interchange format for plant and process information. The twin implementation and pipeline are hosted on GitHub, providing version control and traceability. Deployment utilizes Microsoft Azure services; device connectivity is achieved through a Raspberry‑Pi sensor node, while data acquisition and storage use Azure IoT Hub and Azure Blob storage. Processing and predictive analytics are orchestrated via Azure Functions and Azure Machine Learning services. The exemplar is containerised/virtualised for easy reproducibility and supports evolution through continuous integration pipelines in GitHub Actions. Explicit quality assurance and testing procedures are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Application of the digital twin in this exemplar provides runtime visualization of indoor air quality sensor streams, simulation of airflow scenarios, and predictive analytics for future air quality. These insights support decision making for virus spread prevention, allowing operators to adjust ventilation or occupancy schedules. The documents do not provide explicit confidence metrics or automated recommendation engines, and interaction is primarily through visual dashboards.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The exemplar adopts the AutomationML standard for modeling the plant environment and the twin representation. Other components rely on common industry protocols (e.g., MQTT for message brokering inferred from the ActiveMQ mention in GreenhouseDT, time‑series database InfluxDB, and containerisation via Docker or virtual machines). No explicit certification or compliance framework is referenced.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)

a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.
